petitioner_background: The petitioner is a high-performance athletic trainer and coach in the sport of track and field athletics. He worked with track and field athletes of the Venezuelan national team as a staff member of the Venezuelan athletics federation from 2012 until at least 2017. He seeks classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA § 203(b)(1)(A)) to continue his work as an athletic trainer in the United States.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of several awards he received as a coach and athletic instructor working with track and field athletes of the Venezuelan national team.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded the petitioner did not satisfy this criterion, reasoning that since the petitioner 'appears to be coming to work in the U.S. as a trainer, the awards won as a coach will not apply,' and that 'coaching is a different career than athletic trainer' so the petitioner 'cannot borrow accomplishments earned in another field.' The AAO disagreed, noting the terms 'coach' and 'trainer' have been used interchangeably throughout the record to refer to the petitioner's work, and his duties have consistently been described as including both physical training of individual track and field athletes and providing coaching on techniques and strategies to improve performance. The AAO found the Director erroneously treated coaching and training as two completely different fields and did not conduct a full analysis of the submitted evidence. The matter was remanded for re-evaluation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence that the Venezuelan athletics federation admitted him as a coach of the Venezuelan national team.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined that because the petitioner intends to work as an athletic trainer in the United States, the association 'is not in the [petitioner's] field,' and based on this determination did not further evaluate the petitioner's claims and evidence relating to this criterion. The AAO disagreed with the conclusion that athletic coach and athletic trainer are not within the same area of expertise, and instructed the Director on remand to re-examine the evidence and the petitioner's statements explaining how the evidence supports his eligibility.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a large volume of evidence including online articles about him and his work, along with transcripts of claimed television interviews he had given.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined the petitioner satisfied this criterion, but the AAO found the record did not support this favorable determination. The AAO noted that although a large volume of evidence was submitted, the petitioner did not provide evidence that any of the submitted materials appeared in a qualifying form of media. The record did not contain evidence of the relative circulation, readership or viewership of the publications, and therefore did not demonstrate that the materials appeared in major trade publications or other major media. For the claimed television interviews, the petitioner provided transcripts but did not provide screenshots or links to the interviews or evidence of the dates on which they aired. The AAO instructed the Director to advise the petitioner of these evidentiary deficiencies on remand and allow him an opportunity to submit additional evidence.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted two letters from officials of the Venezuelan athletics federation. One letter from a member of the federation's board of coaches described 'the activities that are part of the annual judging process for coaches.' The second letter from the federation's general secretary explained 'the judging processes in the competition track' and the judging processes that impact administrative decision-making within the organization. The petitioner claimed he met this criterion based on his duties as a member of the federation.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined without elaboration that the petitioner submitted sufficient evidence to meet this criterion, but the AAO withdrew this determination. The AAO found that the first letter described the review and oversight functions of the board of coaches, a distinct component within the federation, rather than individual judging responsibilities performed by all trainers and coaches working for the federation, and neither the letter nor the record established that the petitioner was a member of the board of coaches or charged with performing those review activities. The second letter from the general secretary did not expressly state that the petitioner participated in the described judging activities as a member of the coaching/training staff; rather, she stated the federation has 'rigorous selection processes in each area' and that the petitioner 'goes through this meticulous process every year' to maintain his staff position. The AAO instructed the Director to re-evaluate the evidence and request clarification from the petitioner about the nature of his claimed judging activities.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted recommendation letters from athletes, coaches, and trainers within the Venezuelan athletics federation discussing his original contributions, including a letter from a Venezuelan athlete he claimed to have coached.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director summarily concluded that 'the evidence does not demonstrate the major significance' of the petitioner's original contributions, only vaguely referencing 'recommendation letters' without specifically identifying any documents. The AAO agreed with the petitioner that it was difficult to discern what specific letters the Director considered. Additionally, the Director questioned the credibility of a recommendation letter from a Venezuelan athlete, noting that USCIS independently reviewed her Wikipedia page and found the petitioner was not identified as her coach. The AAO noted that under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(16)(i), if an unfavorable decision is based on derogatory information considered by USCIS of which the petitioner is unaware, the petitioner must be advised of this fact and offered an opportunity to rebut the information before the decision is rendered—and the Director did not afford the petitioner this opportunity. On remand, the Director must re-examine the totality of the evidence and advise the petitioner of any outside information before relying on it.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted documents claiming he had a leading or critical role for the Venezuelan athletics federation, including evidence of the organization's 'historical legacy' as the national governing body of the sport of athletics in Venezuela, its official recognition by the sport's international governing body and the International Olympic Committee, its regulatory authority in the sport, and its contributions to the development of Venezuela's best track and field athletes.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director acknowledged the petitioner submitted documents claiming he had a leading or critical role for the federation, but found that 'it does not appear [the federation] had a distinguished reputation prior to the [petitioner's] alleged tenure with them' because letters indicated the Venezuelan national team was not consistently winning medals in international competition at the time the petitioner was hired. On appeal, the petitioner asserted the evidence showed the federation enjoyed a distinguished reputation during his tenure and beyond, and that there is no specific requirement to demonstrate the organization had a distinguished reputation at an earlier date. The AAO agreed the Director did not provide sufficient analysis of the petitioner's evidence or explanation for concluding he did not meet this criterion, and instructed the Director on remand to re-evaluate the previously submitted evidence and the arguments and evidence submitted on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted an employment contract with the Venezuelan athletics federation for work performed between 2015 and 2017, and an 'income statement' prepared by a Venezuelan public accountant indicating he earned 'professional fees' of $72,000 over a three-year period. The contract stated he was hired to 'give training courses,' to prepare athletes 'technically and physically' for competition, and to 'participate in the programs of...training sports talents in the discipline of Athletics.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded the petitioner did not submit sufficient evidence of his past earnings. The AAO agreed that the employment contract alone did not provide sufficient evidence of past earnings since the claim was based on payments received between 2015 and 2017, so tax returns, pay statements, or other corroborating evidence would reasonably be expected. The AAO noted the income statement did not identify the sources of the petitioner's income, nor was it consistent with the amount offered in the employment contract for the same period. The AAO rejected the petitioner's reliance on USCIS Policy Manual guidance about prospective salary, finding it applied to contracts or job offers showing future compensation rather than claims of past earnings. However, the AAO found the Director erroneously concluded USCIS could not consider the petitioner's prior earnings as an athletic coach because his intended U.S. work was as a trainer rather than coach, since the terms are interchangeable based on the record. On remand, the Director may request additional evidence relating to this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO withdrew the Director's decision and remanded the matter for entry of a new decision. The AAO found that the Director's unfavorable determinations with respect to several evidentiary criteria did not specifically address all of the petitioner's claims or evidence and in some instances did not appear to adhere to USCIS regulations and policy guidance in explaining the reasons for denial. Specifically, the Director improperly treated 'coach' and 'trainer' as entirely separate fields (affecting analysis of the awards, membership, and high salary criteria), provided insufficient analysis of the original contributions and leading role criteria, erroneously concluded the petitioner met the published material and judging criteria without adequate support, and improperly relied on derogatory Wikipedia information without providing the petitioner notice and an opportunity to rebut under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(16)(i). The AAO instructed the Director to issue a new RFE, re-evaluate evidence under all criteria, and if the petitioner satisfies at least three criteria, conduct a final merits determination evaluating sustained national or international acclaim.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537 (AAO 2015)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The Director committed a procedural violation by relying on derogatory information from outside the record—specifically, an independent USCIS review of a Venezuelan athlete's Wikipedia page that purportedly showed the petitioner was not identified as her coach—to question the credibility of a recommendation letter, without advising the petitioner of this information or offering him an opportunity to rebut it before issuing the decision, as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(16)(i). Additionally, the Director's written decision failed to fully explain the specific reasons for denial as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(i) and Matter of M-P-, providing only vague references to 'recommendation letters' without identifying specific documents considered, and failing to conduct complete analyses under several criteria. The Director's previous RFE also did not address the published material criterion or provide notice of the derogatory outside information, so a new RFE must be issued on remand.
